_title: IEC 60364-5-53 / IEC 60898 / IEC 60269 — Protective Device Time/Current Characteristics and Selectivity
_note: Time/current curves define the operating boundaries of protective devices. Designers use these to verify ADS disconnection times, selectivity between upstream and downstream devices, and to calculate Zs_max. All values are for guidance; always verify against manufacturer datasheets for coordination studies.

mcb_characteristics._title: Miniature Circuit Breaker — Type B, C, D Characteristics (IEC 60898-1)
mcb_characteristics._note: MCBs have two independent trip elements: thermal (overload, inverse time) and magnetic (short circuit, instantaneous). The instantaneous range is expressed as multiples of rated current In.

thermal_region_all_types._description: Thermal trip is identical for Types B, C, and D at the same In rating

I1_conventional_non_trip_current.meaning: Device must NOT trip within this time at this current — conventional non-tripping test

I2_conventional_trip_current.meaning: Device MUST trip within this time at this current — conventional tripping test

thermal_region_all_types._implication: At 1.45 × In, the MCB will eventually trip — but it may take up to 1 hour. Do not rely on MCB thermal protection for fast disconnection at mild overloads. Cable Iz must be ≥ I₂/1.45 = In to satisfy the Fundamental Rule.

type_B._description: Type B: magnetic instantaneous trip at 3–5× In. Standard general-purpose MCB for resistive loads, lighting, domestic circuits. Lowest magnetic trip threshold.
type_B.Ia_for_ADS_calculation: 5 × In (upper boundary used to find worst-case Zs_max)
type_B.Zs_max_formula: Zs_max = U₀ / (5 × In)  [U₀ = 230V for most systems]
applications: Circuits with no significant inrush current

type_C._description: Type C: magnetic instantaneous trip at 5–10× In. General-purpose MCB for mixed inductive loads, small motors, transformers with moderate inrush. Most widely used commercial type.

type_D._description: Type D: magnetic instantaneous trip at 10–20× In. High inrush applications: transformers, motors with star-delta starting, welding equipment, large UPS. Not suitable as the only overcurrent device on final circuits — paired with downstream protection for cables.

gG_fuse_characteristics._description: gG fuses are general-purpose current-limiting fuses for full-range protection of cables against overload and short circuit. They have inverse time-current characteristics. The 'g' prefix means full-range breaking capacity; the 'G' suffix means general purpose (as opposed to 'aM' for motor protection).

I2_fusing._note: Higher than MCB (1.45×) — gG fuses are more tolerant of sustained overloads. Cable Iz must satisfy: 1.6 × In ≤ 1.45 × Iz → Iz ≥ 1.10 × In (approximately)

ADS_Zs_max_at_230V._description: Maximum permissible Zs at 230V U₀ for 0.4s disconnection time using gG fuses. Values derived from IEC 60269 time-current curves at t=0.4s (Ia is the operating current at that time).
ADS_Zs_max_at_230V._note: These values are approximate — verify against the specific manufacturer's time-current curve for critical installations.

aM_fuse_characteristics._description: aM (partial range) fuses protect motors against short circuit only — they do NOT protect against overload. Must be paired with a thermal overload relay or motor protection relay for full protection. They have a high inrush tolerance and do not blow on motor starting currents.
aM_fuse_characteristics.use_with: Thermal overload relay (separate device) for overload protection
aM_fuse_characteristics.I2_fusing_conventional: Not rated for conventional overload — no standard I₂/I₁ ratio
typical_applications: DOL starters where separate overload protection is fitted

rcd_trip_characteristics._title: RCD / RCCB Residual Current Trip Times (IEC 61008-1, IEC 61009-1)
rcd_trip_characteristics._note: RCDs are classified by residual current waveform type and by time-delay class. The trip time is measured from the moment the residual current reaches IΔn.

type_AC.description: Detects sinusoidal AC residual current only
type_AC.use: General domestic use — resistive and linear inductive loads. NOT suitable for circuits supplying variable-speed drives, EV chargers, computers, class 1 IT equipment, or any load with DC leakage components.
type_AC.prohibited_for: Circuits with Class A or Class F loads

type_A.description: Detects sinusoidal AC AND pulsating DC residual current
type_A.use: Required wherever Type AC is insufficient: socket outlet circuits that may supply electronic equipment, washing machines, dishwashers, EV charging equipment (Mode 2 and Mode 3). IEC 60364-4-41 AMD1:2017 recommends Type A as minimum for socket outlet circuits.
type_A.note: Mandatory for EV charging Mode 2/3 installations per IEC 60364-7-722

type_F.description: Detects AC, pulsating DC, and high-frequency DC residual current from frequency converters
type_F.use: Circuits supplying variable-speed drive equipment (VSDs/VFDs), fan speed controllers, pump inverters. Provides selectivity against nuisance tripping from high-frequency leakage.

type_B.description: Detects all AC, pulsating DC, and smooth DC residual current
type_B.use: EV charging Mode 3/4 equipment that may produce smooth DC fault current, 3-phase VFDs, medical equipment. Mandatory where smooth DC leakage is possible (IEC 60364-7-722 Clause 722.531.2.101).
type_B.note: More expensive and larger than Type A — specify only where DC fault current is a realistic possibility

S_type_time_delayed._description: Selective (S-type) RCDs for discrimination — installed at distribution board level upstream of socket-outlet RCDs
S_type_time_delayed.use: Installed upstream of final circuit RCDs to prevent upstream tripping on a downstream fault

socket_outlet_RCD_IEC_requirement.note: Socket outlets ≤20A in non-specialist locations require 30mA protection. Many countries mandate 30mA RCD protection on all final circuits.

rcd_trip_characteristics.non_operating_current: 0.5 × IΔn (RCD must NOT trip below this value at any residual current)

selectivity_methods._title: Discrimination / Selectivity between overcurrent protective devices
selectivity_methods._note: Selectivity (discrimination) means that on a fault, only the device directly protecting the faulted circuit operates — upstream devices remain closed. Total selectivity means the upstream device never operates for any fault on the downstream circuit.

current_selectivity.description: Upstream device has a higher rated current so its thermal trip region is above the downstream device's instantaneous trip region. Effective only for overload and modest fault currents.
current_selectivity.condition: Instantaneous trip of downstream device < non-tripping region of upstream device at the same current
current_selectivity.limitation: Difficult to achieve for short-circuit currents — both devices may be in their instantaneous regions

time_selectivity.description: Upstream device has an intentional time delay so downstream device operates first
time_selectivity.typical_approach: Downstream MCB: instantaneous. Upstream MCCB: short-time delay (STD) setting 100–200ms. Most upstream ACBs have adjustable LSIG settings.
time_selectivity.limitation: Time-delay increases energy let-through at the upstream device — cables must have adequate fault withstand

energy_selectivity.description: Current-limiting downstream device clears the fault so quickly (within half-cycle) that the let-through energy I²t is less than the minimum operating I²t of the upstream device
energy_selectivity.when_applicable: Only between current-limiting devices (HRC fuses or current-limiting MCBs/MCCBs)

zone_selective_interlocking.description: Electronic communication between devices: when a downstream device detects a fault, it sends a signal to the upstream device to add a short delay. If the downstream device clears the fault, the upstream device returns to instantaneous. If the downstream device fails, the upstream trips instantly.
zone_selective_interlocking.when_applicable: Only with electronic trip units in MCCBs and ACBs. Requires communication link.
zone_selective_interlocking.advantage: Achieves total selectivity without energy penalty

rcd_selectivity.description: Upstream S-type RCD (130–500ms minimum delay) coordinates with downstream general-type RCDs (≤300ms) and socket-outlet RCDs (≤40ms at IΔn).
rcd_selectivity.typical_arrangement: Consumer unit: 100mA S-type RCD upstream. Final circuit MCBs: 30mA general-type RCBOs.
rcd_selectivity.design_check: S-type minimum non-trip time (130ms) > downstream general type maximum trip time at IΔn (300ms)? Note: this creates an apparent overlap — resolved because the S-type is at a higher IΔn rating (typically 100mA vs 30mA final circuit) so they cannot both see the same fault level.

let_through_energy._reference: IEC 60364-4-43, Clause 434.5 — adiabatic check for cables
let_through_energy._note: The I²t (amperes-squared-seconds) value is the energy let through by a device during fault clearance. This must not exceed the thermal withstand of the protected cable: I²t ≤ k²S². Manufacturer datasheets provide I²t at various prospective fault currents.
let_through_energy.formula: Cable passes if: I²t (device let-through) ≤ k²S²

_worked_example.scenario: 32A Type C MCB protects 4mm² copper PVC cable. PSCC at device = 3kA.
_worked_example.I2t_MCB_typical_at_3kA: Approximately 25000 A²s (from manufacturer datasheet at 3kA prospective)
_worked_example.note: Always obtain I²t from manufacturer datasheet — the value above is illustrative
